title: Prospective associations of sleep duration and screen time with transition from overweight/obesity to normal BMI in U.S. adolescents.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study: 3498 U.S. adolescents aged 9-11 years with overweight/obesity from Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time measured as generic composite (hours/day) from ABCD Youth Screen Time Survey combining TV, videos, games, texting, video chat, social media. No separately reported smartphone/SM-specific coefficient. Violates FT-IC2 requirement for specific smartphone OR social media variable.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in International Journal of Obesity, 2025

FT_EC7_reason: FT_IC2 NOT_MET; does not meet full-text inclusion criteria and therefore FT-EC7 check does not apply
notes: C1 and C2 disagreed on FT_IC2. C1 correctly identified the screen time variable as a generic composite without smartphone/SM-specific breakdown, correctly applying NOT_MET. C2 incorrectly classified this as MET, treating the composite as satisfying the FT_IC2 requirement. The coder prompt explicitly states generic STS composite with no phone/SM breakdown = NOT_MET (violates FT-IC2). This is exclusion via FT-EC2 (exposure failure), not a measurement issue.

title: Social epidemiology of early adolescent problematic screen use in the United States.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study Year 2 (2018-2020), N=8753 adolescents aged 10-14 years

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures three specific smartphone/social media variables: Video Game Addiction Questionnaire (VGAQ), Social Media Addiction Questionnaire (SMAQ), and Mobile Phone Involvement Questionnaire (MPIQ). All are problematic use scales qualifying under FT-IC2.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple linear regression analyses examining associations between sociodemographic predictors and three problematic screen use measures (VGAQ, SMAQ, MPIQ). Table 3 reports B coefficients with 95% CIs and p-values for all associations.

FT_IC4.evidence: Table 3 reports B coefficients with 95% confidence intervals and p-values for associations (e.g., Male vs Female: B=0.74, 95% CI 0.69-0.79, p<0.001 for video games; B=-0.11, 95% CI -0.16 to -0.05, p<0.001 for social media)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis of ABCD data with hypothesis testing and reported associations between sociodemographic factors and problematic screen use

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Pediatric Research, 2022; no duplicate/preprint evidence

FT_EC7_reason: FT-EC7 APPLIES. Problematic video game use, social media use, and mobile phone use are the DEPENDENT VARIABLES in all reported analyses (Table 3). All predictors are ONLY demographic/contextual (age, sex, race/ethnicity, primary language, household income, parental education, parent marital status). The analysis is CROSS-SECTIONAL (not longitudinal). Per rule E7 and prompt criteria (a, b, c), when SM/phone is DV with demographic-only predictors in cross-sectional design, FT-EC7 applies. Note: prompt line 55 states FT-EC7 does NOT apply if paper includes cross-sectional analyses with SM as DV, but this contradicts E7. Following binding E7: cross-sectional with SM as DV = FT-EC7 applies.

notes: C1 correctly identified FT-EC7 applies due to cross-sectional design with SM/phone as DVs and demographic-only predictors. C2 incorrectly stated FT-EC7 does not apply, citing cross-sectional design as the reason. However, per binding edge case E7, cross-sectional analyses with SM as DV trigger FT-EC7 (direction ignored, only demographics matter). Both coders correctly assigned MET to FT_IC1-IC6.

title: Generalizable prediction of childhood ADHD symptoms from neurocognitive testing and youth characteristics.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data from 10,000+ youth enrolled at baseline (ages 9-10) across 21 U.S. sites

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time included as a predictor variable in machine learning models. Measured from ABCD Screen Time Questionnaire and featured prominently in predictive feature importance analysis across PCR and LASSO models. Screen time appears as a meaningful predictor contributing to ADHD symptom prediction.

FT_IC3.evidence: Machine learning regression models (PCR and LASSO) with reported correlation coefficients between predicted and actual ADHD symptoms. Models test screen time as a predictor of ADHD symptoms with reported statistical results (r values and 95% CIs).

FT_IC4.evidence: Reports multiple r values and 95% CIs for model performance across timepoints: 1-year full sample PCR r=0.40, 95% CI 0.38-0.42; LASSO r=0.39, 95% CI 0.37-0.41; 2-year full sample PCR r=0.40, 95% CI 0.38-0.42; LASSO r=0.38, 95% CI 0.36-0.40

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using machine learning predictive modeling (PCR and LASSO) with cross-validation across ABCD sites and left-out test data validation

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Translational Psychiatry, 2023

FT_EC7_reason: FT-EC7 does NOT apply. Screen time is a PREDICTOR (independent variable) of ADHD symptoms (DV), not the dependent variable. Paper examines screen time → ADHD prediction, the opposite directionality from FT-EC7 scope. FT-EC7 applies only when smartphone/SM is the DV being predicted from demographic/contextual factors in longitudinal prospective design.
notes: C1 correctly identified screen time as a predictor (IV) with reported coefficients/statistics in ML models, correctly assigned MET to FT_IC2-IC4, and correctly determined FT-EC7 does not apply. C2 incorrectly assigned FT_IC2 NOT_MET, arguing screen time had no 'separately reported coefficient or analysis isolating smartphone/SM association.' However, per E4 (ML coefficients count), machine learning feature importance and model contributions count as extractable associations. Screen time was a measured predictor with reported model performance (r values), satisfying FT_IC3 and FT_IC4.
